retrieved_docs.title: Wilson Disease -- Evaluation -- Laboratory Studies
retrieved_docs.content: Several potential diagnostic approaches for Wilson disease may be utilized. The diagnostic algorithm typically involves serologic testing, urine testing, and an ocular exam. The first serologic test recommended is serum ceruloplasmin, where a level of <20 mg/dL indicates an abnormality. Ceruloplasmin testing in isolation has a low positive predictive value, ranging from 0.6% to 8%, and a low negative predictive value, ranging from 35% to 50%. [7] Other forms of chronic liver disease may exhibit low serum ceruloplasmin levels. If ceruloplasmin is <20 mg/dL, the patient should undergo a 24-hour urine copper evaluation. A level >40 µg (typically >100 µg) demonstrates an abnormality. A ceruloplasmin level of <5 mg/dL suggests aceruloplasminemia, a disease caused by mutations in the ceruloplasmin gene, resulting in the absence of ceruloplasmin and cerebral iron deposition.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Ceruloplasmin -- Clinical Significance -- Treatment for Aceruloplasmin and Wilson Disease
retrieved_docs.content: Treatment for aceruloplasminemia mainly involves chelation therapy and increasing serum ceruloplasmin. Fresh-frozen plasma, which contains ceruloplasmin combined with IV desferrioxamine, effectively decreases iron content in the liver. Repetitive fresh-frozen plasma treatment can improve neurologic signs/symptoms. Treatment options for Wilson disease include chelation therapy (D-penicillamine, trientine, and tetrathiomolybdate) or zinc salts. Medical therapy is continued lifelong as copper accumulation is not manageable with dietary restriction alone. It is important to note that there may be an associated paradoxical worsening of neurological status after initiation of chelation therapy. While this is not fully understood, studies suggest that increased mobilization of free copper by chelator therapy may lead to acute worsening upon initiation of treatment. [34]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Ceruloplasmin -- Testing
retrieved_docs.content: Wilson disease is diagnosed based on clinical presentation, biochemical tests, and ATP7B mutation analysis. Regarding serum ceruloplasmin testing, only those with low serum ceruloplasmin, low serum copper, and high urine copper meet the criteria for Wilson disease. However, some patients diagnosed with Wilson disease exhibit normal ceruloplasmin levels. When urine and serum concentrations of copper and ceruloplasmin are low, the patient suffers from a copper deficiency. Factors interfering with the body's ability to metabolize copper, such as chelator medications, diet, and iron restriction, may also affect serum ceruloplasmin levels. [26] [27] See Image. Ceruloplasmin Interpretation.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Ceruloplasmin -- Testing -- Deficiency States
retrieved_docs.content: Low ceruloplasmin levels may indicate the following pathological conditions: Wilson disease, Menke disease, copper deficiency, aceruloplasminemia, or in states of low protein energy intake (eg, malnutrition.) [21] [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Wilson Disease -- Evaluation -- Leipzig Scoring
retrieved_docs.content: The Leipzig scoring system was previously proposed, which includes the presence of typical clinical signs and symptoms, including KF rings, neurologic symptoms, serum ceruloplasmin, and Coombs-negative hemolytic anemia, as well as other tests, including hepatic copper quantification, urinary copper excretion (in the absence of acute hepatitis), and mutation analysis. A composite score of 4 or higher establishes the diagnosis. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Ceruloplasmin -- Clinical Significance -- Wilson disease
retrieved_docs.content: Wilson disease presents between ages 5 to 35 . [38] The clinical presentation may include the following symptoms: Hepatolenticular degeneration Kayser-Fleischer rings Parkinsonism Ataxia Personality changes Depression Psychosis Acute liver failure Chronic liver failure Cirrhosis Renal issues (renal tubular acidosis with aminoaciduria) Secondary Hemolysis is less common Most patients with Wilson disease have low ceruloplasmin levels Treatment may include: Penicillamine Trientine Zinc

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Ceruloplasmin -- Testing
retrieved_docs.content: Standard Ceruloplasmin measurement is via a blood test, often by analyzing a serum sample with immunoassays, immunoturbidimetry, or immunonephelometry. The sample must be spun and separated as soon as the laboratory receives it and assays it promptly. The samples may be stored at 4 degrees Celsius for up to three days or longer at -70 degrees Celsius. The serum test is often part of the workup when there is suspicion of Wilson disease and is commonly ordered along with urine copper levels. Several factors, including diet, hormone levels, and other genetic disorders, may influence the resulting ceruloplasmin levels in the urine and serum. [19] [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Wilson Disease -- Treatment / Management -- Assessing Response to Therapy
retrieved_docs.content: Monitoring during chelation therapy is recommended and involves regular evaluation of 24-hour urine copper excretion, liver biochemistries, INR/PT, complete blood count, routine urinalysis, serum copper levels, and ceruloplasmin.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Ceruloplasmin -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Ceruloplasmin is a serum ferroxidase responsible for 95% copper transport in the blood. It is well known for its role in the pathogenesis of Wilson disease and regulating iron metabolism. [1] Also, ceruloplasmin is a positive acute-phase reactant, meaning its levels increase in inflammatory conditions or cell injury. [2] Copper is a trace element that acts as a cofactor for multiple enzymes in critical cellular reactions, such as cytochromes, which participate in the electron transport chain and redox catalysis. [3] Recent studies have shown that alterations of ceruloplasmin protein and copper homeostasis are associated with Alzheimer disease. [4] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Liver Function Tests -- Clinical Significance -- Wilson Disease
retrieved_docs.content: Wilson disease, a rare autosomal-recessive inherited disorder of copper metabolism, is characterized by excess copper deposition in the liver, brain, and other tissues. It is fatal if not recognized and treated early. A low serum ceruloplasmin level is seen in the majority (up to 85%) of the cases. [43] Kayser-Fleischer rings can be a clinical clue but are not always present. The 24-hour urinary copper excretion test is usually abnormal, with more than 100 micrograms of copper excretion in the urine indicating Wilson's disease. A liver biopsy remains the confirmatory test. [44]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Ceruloplasmin -- Pathophysiology -- Special Situations
retrieved_docs.content: Wilson disease results from an autosomal recessive mutation in the ATP7B protein. Mutations in ATP7B (located on chromosome 13) lead to Wilson disease. ATP7B is an efflux transporter in the liver, but it is also essential in the Trans-Golgi Network for transferring copper for the metalation of ceruloplasmin and biliary copper excretion. [32] The point mutation H1069Q is the most common ATP7B mutation in patients from Central, Eastern, and Northern Europe, and 50 to 80% of Wilson disease patients from these countries carry at least one H1069Q allele. [33] Mutations resulting in completely absent or non-functional ATP7B protein activity are associated with early-onset, typically hepatic, severe Wilson disease; these mutations are comparatively rare. [34] It is characterized by hepatolenticular degeneration due to copper deposition in organs, including the basal ganglia, cornea, and liver. In Wilson's disease, ceruloplasmin levels are usually low, but urinary excretion of copper is high. Liver biopsy results show high copper content. Clinically, a dark ring around the iris suggests a diagnosis of Wilson disease. This sign is known as a Kayser-Fleischer ring. [35]

retrieved_docs.title: Penicillamine -- Monitoring
retrieved_docs.content: According to the American Association for the Study of Liver Diseases (AASLD), the adequacy of therapy in Wilson disease is monitored by evaluating clinical and biochemical improvement and measuring 24-hour urinary copper excretion during treatment. Urinary copper excretion is highest immediately after starting therapy and may surpass 1,000 to 2,000 μg daily. During maintenance therapy, urinary copper excretion should typically be approximately 200 to 500 μg daily. Serum non-ceruloplasmin-bound copper levels normalize with effective treatment. In patients previously excreting 200 to 500 μg of copper daily, values exceeding 500 μg daily suggest either nonadherence to penicillamine or increased copper intake. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Liver Disease -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Wilson disease: Raised urine copper, decreased serum ceruloplasmin, and liver biopsy. Genetic testing for the ATP7B gene.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Ceruloplasmin -- Clinical Significance -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Surveillance should include yearly glucose tolerance tests starting at the age of 15 or the age of diagnosis if, after 15 years old, to monitor for the inception of diabetes mellitus. Regular eye exams should also be a consideration, as retinopathy is a prominent feature in aceruloplasminemia and Wilson disease. An annual echocardiogram, thyroid and liver function evaluation, and a complete blood count (CBC) starting at the diagnosis should be performed to monitor for other potential complications. Blood transfusions should be approached cautiously as the increased iron load may be challenging for patients with these disorders. In patients with Wilson disease, it is strongly recommended that familial screening is performed. The American Association for Study of Liver Diseases and the European Association for the Study of Liver both separately recommend screening first-degree relatives of the affected individuals, suggesting siblings or offspring only. [37] While most patients with Wilson disease who become pregnant have successful pregnancies, a discussion is necessary about the potential side effects of anti-copper therapy, as all available anti-copper drugs can pass into the breast.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Ceruloplasmin -- Pathophysiology -- Special Situations
retrieved_docs.content: Menkes disease is an X-linked recessive disorder caused by a mutation in the ATP7A protein. With this mutation, copper cannot be transported out of the GI tract; therefore, copper is unavailable to the liver, which causes low ceruloplasmin levels. The patients present in infancy have sparse, brittle, and kinky hair, growth retardation and neurologic degradation, and death if untreated in the first few years of life. [31]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Ceruloplasmin -- Testing -- Age/Interval(mg/L)
retrieved_docs.content: Cord (term): 50 to 330 Birth to 4 months: 150 to 560 5 to 6 months: 260 to 830 7 to 36 months: 310 to 900 6 to 12 years: 250 to 450

retrieved_docs.content: of anemia. By incorporating folic acid into multivitamin‑iron supplements, clinicians aim to correct or avert folate deficiency, support normal erythropoiesis, and improve overall hematologic health while simultaneously providing other essential vitamins (A, C, D, E, B‑complex) and minerals (iron, calcium, magnesium, zinc, copper, iodine) that contribute to broader metabolic and physiological functions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 1 -- Community 121
retrieved_docs.content: formulation design, laboratory evaluation, and patient‑centered nutritional management, and supports clinicians in selecting appropriate supplementation to prevent or treat nutritional deficiencies such as vitamin C deficiency.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2027
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin B12, encompassing cyanocobalamin and its analogues, includes several distinct cobalamin forms such as mecobalamin and hydroxokobalamin, which are recognized as member compounds within this vitamin family.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 1 -- Community 184
retrieved_docs.content: and manganese is noted, suggesting formulation considerations. Collectively, these multivitamin‑iron and zinc products aim to improve hematopoiesis, correct simultaneous micronutrient deficiencies, enhance iron absorption (often facilitated by vitamin C and chelated iron forms), reduce gastrointestinal side effects, and provide comprehensive metabolic support for patients at risk of iron‑deficiency anemia, folate‑deficiency anemia, combined nutritional deficits, or those requiring bariatric supplementation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2341
retrieved_docs.content: Failure (biologic function) is associated with الفَشَلٌ الكَبِدِيُّ الكُلْوِيّ, indicating that a general loss of biological function can be linked to hepatorenal failure—a severe condition in which both liver and kidney functions are compromised. Recognizing this association is important for clinicians, as it highlights the need to assess and manage systemic organ failure comprehensively in affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 1 -- Community 185
retrieved_docs.content: (نَزْف المسالك التنفسية الوَليدِيّ). Laboratory findings that fall below reference ranges, particularly low total hemoglobin concentration, are interpreted as indicative of hemolytic or perinatal jaundice, underscoring the diagnostic importance of quantitative testing in this age group. Collectively, these relationships illustrate that the neonatal period serves as a critical temporal nexus linking infectious, metabolic, hematologic, and hemorrhagic conditions—many organized under “other perinatal conditions” or “hemolytic‑jaundice” categories—and that recognizing these interconnections is essential for accurate assessment, diagnosis, and management of newborns.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 0 -- Community 18
retrieved_docs.content: stage. Recognizing these interconnections, the associated syndromes, and the relevant laboratory parameters (e.g., total hemoglobin) is essential for accurate diagnosis, interpretation of test results, and targeted management of infants during the neonatal and early infancy periods.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 155
retrieved_docs.content: entities illustrate the diverse therapeutic roles of magnesium salts, iron compounds and fibrates, while emphasizing common safety considerations—particularly the avoidance of magnesium‑based agents and fibrates in patients with renal impairment and the avoidance of fibrates in hepatic steatosis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 2 -- Community 377
retrieved_docs.content: Cholin is presented as a broader nutrient category that includes vitamins, indicating that vitamins are a sub‑group within the cholin family. Specific vitamins such as riboflavin and the vitamin B complex are identified as child entities of the vitamin group, while vitamin K 2 and vitamin K 3 are classified as individual members of the vitamin category. Formulations described as Multivitamin+mineral preparations, Multivitamin agent, and Multivitamins with minerals are all recognized as types of vitamins, and the extensive prenatal multivitamin product (VITAMIN A ACETATE 2000 [iU] / BETA CAROTENE 2000 [iU] / ASCORBIC ACID 120 mg / CHOLECALCIFEROL 400 [iU] / ALPHA‑TOCOPHEROL ACETATE 22 mg / THIAMINE MONONITRATE 1.84 mg / RIBOFLAVIN 3 mg / NIACINAMIDE 20 mg / PYRIDOXINE HYDROCHLORIDE 10 mg / FOLIC ACID 1 mg / CYANOCOBALAMIN 12 µg / CALCIUM CARBONATE 200 mg / FERROUS FUMARATE 27 mg / ZINC OXIDE 25 mg / CUPRIC OXIDE 2 mg) is specifically linked to the vitamin group, reflecting its composition of multiple vitamins (A, beta‑carotene, C, D, E, thiamine, riboflavin, niacin, pyridoxine, folic acid, cyanocobalamin) together with essential minerals (calcium, iron, zinc, copper). The entity “minerály” (minerals) is noted as related to “vitaminy” (vitamins), underscoring the interdependence of these micronutrients. Both vitamins and minerals are further connected to the concept of nutritional deficiency (نقص التغذية), highlighting their importance in preventing and addressing deficiencies in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 1 -- Community 73
retrieved_docs.content: disorders are placed under the broader category of blood‑globulin deficiency (نقص غلوبيولين الدم), indicating that globulin deficiency underlies or is related to various immune system abnormalities. Recognizing malaise, fatigue, and the link between immunity disorders and globulin deficiency, together with the shared pharmacologic strategies and contraindications, supports an integrated diagnostic and therapeutic approach in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 1326
retrieved_docs.content: Acitretin, a systemic retinoid medication, is contraindicated in patients who have a liver disorder (اِضطرَاب الكَبِد), as the drug can cause hepatotoxic effects and exacerbate hepatic dysfunction; therefore, clinicians should avoid prescribing acitretin to individuals with existing liver disease to ensure safe patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2026
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin B1, plain is recognized as a form of vitamin B1 that may be administered alone or together with vitamins B6 and B12, indicating that it can be used both as a single‑nutrient supplement and as part of combination vitamin preparations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1825
retrieved_docs.content: Immunity disorders are classified as a subset of نقص غلوبيولين الدم, which refers to a deficiency of blood globulins. This globulin deficiency results in reduced levels of immunoglobulins, compromising the body’s immune defenses and predisposing individuals to infections and other immune‑related complications. Recognizing immunity disorders within the broader context of نقص غلوبيولين الدم helps clinicians understand the underlying immunological deficit and guides appropriate diagnostic evaluation and therapeutic strategies aimed at restoring adequate immune function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 1301
retrieved_docs.content: Various alimentary tract and metabolism products constitute a broad class of compounds that influence gastrointestinal function and metabolic pathways. Within this class, the antioxidant lipoic acid (kyselina lipoová) is included, reflecting its role in metabolic processes. Additionally, several pharmacologic agents are categorized as members of this group: migalastat, a pharmacological chaperone used for Fabry disease; miglustat, an inhibitor of glucosylceramide synthase employed in the treatment of Gaucher disease and Niemann‑Pick type C; nitisinone, an inhibitor of 4‑hydroxyphenylpyruvate dioxygenase used for hereditary tyrosinemia type 1; sapropterin, a synthetic form of tetrahydrobiopterin indicated for phenylketonuria; and trientine, a copper‑chelating agent used in Wilson disease. Collectively, these substances are recognized as various alimentary tract and metabolism products, highlighting their shared relevance to digestive and metabolic health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 1 -- Community 30
retrieved_docs.content: B₁₂ family, mecobalamin and hydroxocobalamin are members of “Vitamin B12 (cyanocobalamin and analogues),” reflecting alternative cobalamin analogues used in supplementation and therapy. Overall, the taxonomy connects specific vitamin forms, iron compounds, and combined supplement products to the categories of antianemic therapy, iron supplementation, and the prevention or treatment of nutritional deficiencies. This structured framework enables clinicians to select formulations that simultaneously address iron deficiency, vitamin‑dependent metabolic needs, and broader micronutrient gaps, particularly in populations with increased iron requirements, malabsorption risk, or postoperative bariatric status.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1489
retrieved_docs.content: Cholin is presented as a broader category that encompasses the entity vitaminy. Within this vitamin group, specific members include vitamin K 2 and vitamin K 3, as well as formulations such as Multivitamin agent and Multivitamins with minerals. All of these vitamin-related entities are linked to نقص التغذية (nutritional deficiency), indicating that deficiencies in these vitamins or multivitamin preparations are relevant to the clinical understanding of nutrient‑related health issues.
